items.q: How did Hitler come to power in 1933?
items.correct: He was legally appointed Chancellor by President Hindenburg as part of a conservative political deal, after the Nazis became the largest party in the Reichstag.
items.misconception: Hitler seized power through a violent coup or revolution.
items.random_wrong: Hitler won an outright majority in the 1932 presidential election.
items.why: The 1923 Beer Hall Putsch failed; in 1933 Hitler came to power constitutionally. Students confuse Nazi violence with the actual mechanism of takeover, which was legal manoeuvring.

items.q: What was the political significance of the Reichstag Fire?
items.correct: Hitler used it to issue the Reichstag Fire Decree suspending civil liberties, jail Communists, and create the conditions for the Enabling Act of March 1933.
items.misconception: Hitler personally set the fire and blamed it on the Communists.
items.random_wrong: The fire destroyed all of Berlin and forced people to support the Nazis.
items.why: Historians still debate who started it; van der Lubbe likely acted alone. What matters is how Hitler exploited it — a key step in the legal slide to dictatorship.

items.q: How effective was Nazi propaganda in winning popular support?
items.correct: Most effective where it reinforced existing views (anti-Versailles, fear of communism, national pride); less so when contradicting daily experience or established religious belief.
items.misconception: Nazi propaganda was so powerful that Germans believed whatever Hitler said.
items.random_wrong: Goebbels invented television specifically to broadcast Nazi propaganda.
items.why: Propaganda works with the grain of existing beliefs. Students imagine Germans as brainwashed; the reality was selective consent reinforced by terror against dissent.

items.q: Which Article of Versailles forced Germany to accept war guilt?
items.why: Correct answer: Article 231. “Article 48” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What ended the period of Weimar stability?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Wall Street Crash 1929. “Munich Putsch” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Enabling Act give Hitler dictatorial powers?
items.why: The real figure is 1933 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
